presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: I've had a cough for months that won't go away
I've lost a lot of weight without trying
Persistent cough and I'm getting more breathless
I feel exhausted all the time and my cough is getting worse

history_data.hpi: Patient presents with a 4-month history of persistent dry cough that has recently become productive of blood-streaked sputum. Over the past 6 weeks the patient has noticed progressive unintentional weight loss of approximately 8kg, worsening fatigue, and gradually increasing dyspnoea on exertion. Appetite has been markedly reduced. No recent fever or purulent sputum to suggest acute infection. Patient is a heavy smoker with a 50 pack-year history and has background COPD. No history of previous malignancy.
patient_quotes: This cough has been going on for months — I just assumed it was my COPD.
I've noticed blood mixed in with the phlegm a few times.
I've lost nearly a stone in weight without even trying.
I'm exhausted all the time — I have no energy.
I'm getting more breathless than usual, even for me.
pmh_pool: COPD — GOLD stage II, diagnosed 6 years ago
Heavy smoking history — 50 pack-years, current smoker
Previous pneumonia — 4 years ago, right lower lobe, fully resolved
Asbestos exposure — worked in shipbuilding for 20 years

social_history.smoking: Current smoker — 50 pack-year history, approximately 25 cigarettes per day for 40 years
social_history.exercise: Minimal — limited by dyspnoea at baseline

negative_history: No recent foreign travel or TB contacts
No bone pain or headache suggesting metastatic disease
No recent chest trauma or surgery

general_appearance: No acute distress at rest but breathless on minimal exertion
respiratory: Reduced breath sounds over right upper zone
Localised monophonic wheeze — right upper lobe
Dullness to percussion right upper zone
lymph_nodes: Right supraclavicular lymphadenopathy — single firm, non-tender node approximately 1.5cm

investigations.Chest X-Ray: Irregular spiculated mass 4.5cm in the right upper lobe. Associated right hilar enlargement — consistent with hilar lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No evidence of bony metastases on the visualised ribs. Appearances are highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy. Urgent CT chest required.
investigations.CT Chest: 4.8cm spiculated right upper lobe mass with irregular margins and pleural tethering. Ipsilateral right hilar and right paratracheal lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion. No pericardial effusion. Highly suspicious for primary bronchogenic carcinoma. CT-guided biopsy and PET staging recommended.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 10.2 × 10⁹/L, Hb 10.4 g/dL (normocytic anaemia — anaemia of chronic disease), MCV 84 fL, Platelets 448 × 10⁹/L (thrombocytosis — paraneoplastic or reactive). Anaemia warrants iron studies and B12/folate assessment.
investigations.CRP: 64 mg/L — elevated, consistent with malignancy-associated inflammation.
investigations.ESR: 88 mm/hr — significantly elevated, consistent with active malignancy.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 128 mmol/L (hyponatraemia — may indicate SIADH, a paraneoplastic syndrome particularly associated with small cell lung cancer), K 3.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, Urea 6.2 mmol/L. Hyponatraemia requires urgent investigation.
investigations.LFTs (Liver Function Tests): ALP 198 IU/L (elevated), ALT 44 IU/L (mildly elevated), Bilirubin 14 µmol/L, Albumin 28 g/L (hypoalbuminaemia — consistent with malnutrition and chronic disease). Elevated ALP raises concern for hepatic or bony metastases.
investigations.Sputum Culture: No significant bacterial growth. Sputum cytology sent separately — result pending. Sputum cytology has moderate sensitivity for central tumours.

hidden_diagnosis: Primary Lung Cancer — Suspected Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (NSCLC), Right Upper Lobe

patient_rules.emotional_state: anxious and worried — aware symptoms are serious, concerned about what investigations may reveal

essential_questions: duration of cough — weeks to months is a red flag
character of cough — dry, productive, blood-streaked
haemoptysis — volume, frequency, frank blood vs streaked
unintentional weight loss — amount and timeframe
fatigue and anorexia — systemic features of malignancy
smoking history — pack-years, current or ex-smoker
previous malignancy or family history of lung cancer
symptoms of metastatic disease — bone pain, headache, confusion, focal neurology
COPD background — change from usual symptoms
night sweats — consider TB or lymphoma as differential
hoarseness — suggests recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement
facial swelling or arm oedema — superior vena cava obstruction
shoulder or arm pain — Pancoast tumour involvement of brachial plexus
essential_examinations: general assessment — cachexia, pallor, tar staining, clubbing
lymph node examination — supraclavicular, cervical, axillary nodes
respiratory examination — focal signs: reduced air entry, localised wheeze, dullness
neurological assessment — focal deficits if cerebral metastases
assessment for superior vena cava obstruction — facial plethora, neck vein distension
critical_actions: recognise lung cancer red flag symptoms: haemoptysis + weight loss + chronic cough + heavy smoking = urgent 2-week-wait referral
obtain urgent chest X-ray — first-line imaging
if CXR suspicious, arrange urgent CT chest (with contrast) for full characterisation
refer urgently to respiratory/thoracic oncology team — 2-week-wait pathway
obtain tissue diagnosis — CT-guided biopsy, bronchoscopy, or endobronchial ultrasound (EBUS) depending on location
staging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and PET-CT to assess for metastatic disease
assess for paraneoplastic syndromes — hyponatraemia (SIADH in SCLC), hypercalcaemia (squamous cell), Cushing syndrome (SCLC)
assess performance status (ECOG/WHO) — guides treatment eligibility
smoking cessation — even at this stage, cessation improves treatment outcomes
bone profile and LFTs to screen for metastatic disease
multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion before any treatment decision
red_flags: Haemoptysis in a smoker over 40 — urgent investigation mandatory
Unintentional weight loss >5% body weight over 3–6 months
Persistent cough lasting more than 3 weeks in a smoker
Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy — suggests N3 disease or metastatic spread
Hyponatraemia — may indicate SIADH (paraneoplastic, especially SCLC)
Elevated ALP or LFTs — may indicate hepatic or bony metastases
Localised monophonic wheeze — endobronchial lesion causing partial obstruction
Hoarseness — recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement by mediastinal disease
Facial swelling and arm oedema — superior vena cava obstruction (oncological emergency)
acceptable_differentials: Pulmonary tuberculosis — chronic cough, haemoptysis, weight loss, night sweats; exclude with sputum AFB and Mantoux/IGRA
Metastatic cancer — secondary lung deposits from breast, colon, renal primary; usually multiple lesions
Lymphoma — mediastinal mass, B symptoms; biopsy required to differentiate
Lung abscess — cavitating lesion, fever, purulent sputum, unwell; CT will differentiate
COPD exacerbation — background COPD but does not explain mass or weight loss
Pneumonia with slow resolution — consolidation, but no acute infection features here and chronic course makes this unlikely
management_actions: Urgent chest X-ray — if not already done
CT chest with contrast — characterise mass, assess mediastinum and lymph nodes
CT abdomen and pelvis — staging, assess for hepatic and adrenal metastases
PET-CT — full staging for surgical or radical treatment candidacy
Tissue biopsy — CT-guided biopsy (peripheral lesion) or bronchoscopy/EBUS (central or hilar lesion)
Sputum cytology — low sensitivity but non-invasive; useful for central tumours
FBC, LFTs, bone profile, U&E — baseline labs and screen for metastatic disease and paraneoplastic syndromes
Performance status assessment (ECOG score) — determines treatment eligibility
Lung function tests (spirometry) — required before surgical resection consideration
Nutritional support and dietitian referral — significant weight loss and hypoalbuminaemia
Palliation of symptoms — cough suppression, analgesia, dyspnoea management as appropriate
Psychological support and patient education about diagnosis pathway
critical_history_questions: How long have you had this cough — has it changed recently?
Have you coughed up any blood — how much and how often?
How much weight have you lost? Over what period of time?
Do you feel more tired than usual?
Has your breathing changed from your usual baseline?
Do you smoke or have you ever smoked? How many cigarettes and for how many years?
Have you ever been exposed to asbestos, dust, or chemicals at work?
Have you had any previous cancer diagnosis?
Is there any family history of lung cancer or other cancers?
Any bone pain, headaches, or confusion?
Any hoarseness or change in your voice?
Any swelling of your face or arms?
Have you had a recent chest X-ray or any previous chest imaging?
